summary: Jeff Atwood discusses upgrading to 24 GB of RAM in his desktop, made affordable by declining DDR3 prices and Intel's Core i7 triple-channel architecture. He debunks the famous misquote about Bill Gates saying '640K ought to be enough for anybody,' while noting Gates did acknowledge underestimating how quickly 640K would become limiting. Atwood argues that cheap, abundant RAM changes how developers think about memory—from a scarce resource requiring careful management to something plentiful enough to ignore. The post reflects on how quickly computing resources that seem excessive become standard.
key_points: Bill Gates never actually said '640K ought to be enough for anybody'—it's a persistent misattributed quote
The Core i7's triple-channel memory architecture enabled affordable 24GB configurations at roughly $12.50 per gigabyte
Having abundant RAM fundamentally changes the developer mindset from careful resource management to treating memory as effectively unlimited
What seems extravagant today (24GB desktop RAM) was recently server-class hardware (Stack Overflow's first database server)
Even Bill Gates, who designed the 640K limit, only expected it to last 10 years—it lasted 6
money_quotes: Algorithms are for people who don't know how to buy RAM.
I've said some stupid things and some wrong things, but not that. No one involved in computers would ever say that a certain amount of memory is enough for all time.
To me, it's more about no longer needing to think about memory as a scarce resource, something you allocate carefully and manage with great care. There's just... lots.
I have to say that in 1981, making those decisions, I felt like I was providing enough freedom for 10 years.
I guess having 24 gigabytes of system memory is a little extravagant, but at these prices – why not?
Remember when one gigabyte of system memory was considered a lot?
I mean, 24 GB of memory should be enough for anybody… right?
key_insight: Abundant, cheap hardware doesn't just make computers faster—it fundamentally changes how programmers think about and approach problems.
